revision.text:
[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|right|300px]]
'''Kitano Tenmangu''' (北野天満宮) is a [[Jinja (Shinto)|Shinto shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], dedicated to Tenjin-san, the ''[[kami]]'' of scholarship. It was built in [[942]], to appease the angry spirit of scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of Tenjin (Heavenly Deity).
The grounds are filled with Sugawara's favorite tree, the plum (''[[ume]]''), and is popular with students praying for success in exams. On the 25th of every month, the shrine plays host to a flea market.
[[ja:北野天満宮]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Temples and Shrines of Kyoto]]

[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|right|300px]]

The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of Tenjin (Heavenly Deity).
The grounds are filled with Sugawara's favorite tree, the plum (''[[ume]]''), and is popular with students praying for success in exams. On the 25th of every month, the shrine plays host to a flea market.
[[ja:北野天満宮]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Temples and Shrines of Kyoto]]

The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of Tenjin (Heavenly Deity).
The grounds are filled with Sugawara's favorite tree, the ''[[ume]]'', and during the blooming season of the plum blossoms the shrine is often very crowded. Kitano-tenmangu is popular with students praying for success in exams. On the 25th of every month, the shrine plays host to a [[flea market]], similar to that of [[To-ji]] temple. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Toji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather.

[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|right|300px]]
'''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Jinja (Shinto)|Shinto shrine]] in Kamigyo Ward, [[Kyoto]], dedicated to [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin-san]], the ''[[kami]]'' of scholarship. It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of Tenjin (Heavenly Deity).
The grounds are filled with Sugawara's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and during the blooming season of the ume blossoms the shrine is often very crowded. The "Baka-sai," or the Plum Blossom Festival, is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[geiko]] and apprentice maiko from [[Kamishichiken]] district. The festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years, in order to mark the death of Michizane. The origin of the outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by Hideyoshi Toyotomi.
Kitano-tenmangu is popular with students praying for success in exams. On the 25th of every month, the shrine plays host to a [[flea market]], similar to that of [[Tō-ji]] temple. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Toji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather.
== External links == [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English)

[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|right|300px]]
'''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Jinja (Shinto)|Shinto shrine]] in Kamigyo Ward, [[Kyoto]], dedicated to [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin-san]], the ''[[kami]]'' of scholarship. It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of Tenjin (Heavenly Deity).
The grounds are filled with Sugawara's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and during the blooming season of the ume blossoms the shrine is often very crowded. The "Baka-sai," or the Plum Blossom Festival, is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[geiko]] and apprentice maiko from [[Kamishichiken]] district for ¥1,500. The festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years, in order to mark the death of Michizane. The origin of the outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by Hideyoshi Toyotomi.
Kitano-tenmangu is popular with students praying for success in exams. On the 25th of every month, the shrine plays host to a [[flea market]], similar to that of [[Tō-ji]] temple. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Toji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather.

[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|right|300px]]
'''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Jinja (Shinto)|Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|Kamigyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], dedicated to [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]]. It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of Tenjin (Heavenly Deity).
The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and during the blooming season of the ume blossoms the shrine is often very crowded. The "Baika-sai," or the Plum Blossom Festival, is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years, in order to mark the death of Michizane. The origin of the outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Hideyoshi Toyotomi]].
Kitano-tenmangu is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine plays host to a [[flea market]], similar to that of [[Tō-ji]] temple. [[Image:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|thumb|right|Prayer hall.]] [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]].
The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]].
Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather.

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] shrine | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = [[947]] | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = 075-461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine was dedicated to him, and in [[986]], he was deified and conferred the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]]. The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. == External links == * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coor title dms|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} [[Category:10th century architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''haiden'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''haiden'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. ==History== It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''haiden'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} == External links == * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} == External links == * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] {{commonscat|Kitano-tenmangu}} [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]] [[zh:北野天滿宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} == External links == * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] {{commonscat|Kitano-tenmangu}} [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]] [[zh:北野天滿宮]]

The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival is held on February 25. Open-air tea ceremonies are hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district. The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] == External links == {{commonscat|Kitano-tenmangu}} * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[ko:기타노텐만구]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]] [[zh:北野天滿宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[Image:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|right|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] == External links == {{commonscat|Kitano-tenmangu}} * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[ko:기타노텐만구]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]] [[zh:北野天滿宮]]

On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] == External links == {{commonscat|Kitano-tenmangu}} * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[ko:기타노텐만구]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]] [[zh:北野天滿宮]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu Kyoto Japan41s3s4592.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[Honden|honden]]'', or main building. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[File:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|thumb|The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall.]] [[File:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|150px|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] == External links == {{commonscat|Kitano-tenmangu}} * [http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/eigo/index.html Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[de:Kitano Tenman-gū]] [[ko:기타노텐만구]] [[it:Santuario Kitano]] [[ja:北野天満宮]] [[zh:北野天滿宮]]

The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu Kyoto Japan41s3s4592.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main building. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[File:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|thumb|The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall.]] [[File:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|150px|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu Kyoto Japan41s3s4592.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main building. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[File:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|thumb|The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall.]] [[File:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|150px|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[File:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|thumb|The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall.]] [[File:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|150px|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] It was built in [[947]], to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in [[986]], the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu Kyoto Japan41s3s4592.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main building. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Bakuro-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81 (075) 461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[File:Kitano-tenmangu haiden.jpg|thumb|The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or main prayer hall.]] [[File:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|150px|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] It was built in 947, to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in 986, the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[Maiko (geiko)|maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] == External links == {{commons category|Kitano-tenmangu}} * [http://kitanotenmangu.or.jp/english/ Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitano Tenman-Gu}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Dazaifu, Fukuoka]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kitano Tenman-gū
北野天満宮 | image = Kitano-tenmangu Kyoto Japan41s3s4592.jpg | width = | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main building. | type = [[Tenman-gū]] | dedication = [[Sugawara no Michizane]] | founded = 947 | closed = | founder = | priest = | reisai = Reitaisai (August 4th) | honden = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | address = Hakuraku-chō, [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]] | phone = +81-75-461-0005 | website = http://www.kitanotenmangu.or.jp/ }} '''{{nihongo|Kitano Tenman-gū|北野天満宮|}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 194-220. ==History== [[File:Plum trees Kitano Tenmangu.jpg.jpg|thumb|Two women praying in front of the ''chishusha'' (地主社)]] [[File:Kitano Tenmangu-3849.JPG|thumb|Lanterns hang from the eaves of the main buildings.]] The shrine was first built in 947 to appease the angry spirit of bureaucrat, scholar and poet [[Sugawara no Michizane]], who had been exiled as a result of political maneuvers of his enemies in the [[Fujiwara clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These messenger, called ''heihaku'', were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list — including Kitano.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies in Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, the Kitano Tenman-gū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Tenjin== The shrine was dedicated to Michizane; and in 986, the scholar-bureaucrat was deified and the title of [[Tenjin (kami)|Tenjin (Heavenly Deity)]] was conferred. [[File:Maiko serving tea at Kitano Tenmangū 2011-02-25.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A [[Maiko (geiko)|maiko]] serving tea at the plum blossom festival.]] The grounds are filled with Michizane's favorite tree, the red and white ''[[ume]]'' or plum blossom, and when they blossom the shrine is often very crowded. The Plum Blossom Festival (梅花祭, ''baikasai'') is held on February 25, coinciding with the monthly market. An open-air tea ceremony (野点, ''nodate'') is hosted by [[Geisha|geiko]] and apprentice maiko from the nearby [[Kamishichiken]] district, where tea and [[wagashi]] are served to 3,000 guests by geisha and maiko.[http://www.kyoto.travel/baika-sai_plum_festival.html Baika-sai (Plum Festival)], Kyoto Travel Guide[http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Open-Air Tea Ceremony with the Scent of Plum Blossoms: Plum Blossom Festival at Kitano Tenman-gu Shrine], Kyoto Shimbun, 2007.2.25 The plum festival has been held on the same day every year for about 900 years to mark the death of Michizane. The outdoor tea ceremony dates back to 1952. In that year, a big festival was held to mark the 1,050th anniversary of Michizane's death, based on the historic Kitano Ochakai tea ceremony hosted at the shrine by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. [[File:Tenmangu flea market 25 August 2006.jpg|thumb|The proverbial flea market at Tenmangū]] Kitano Tenmangū is popular with students praying for success in exams because the deity was in his life a man of literature and knowledge. On the 25th of every month, the shrine hosts a [[flea market]]. Together with the similar festival at [[Tō-ji]], a temple in the same city, they inspired the Kyoto proverb, "Fair weather at the Tōji market means rainy weather at the Tenjin market," calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] == External links == {{commons category|Kitano-tenmangu}} * [http://kitanotenmangu.or.jp/english/ Kitano Tenman-gū Official web page] (In English) * [http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/kp/topics/eng/2007feb/02-25.html Kyoto Shimbun: Open-Air Tea Ceremony...] (in English) {{coord|35|01|52|N|135|44|07|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitano Tenman-Gu}} [[Category:940s architecture]] [[Category:947 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

[http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society.
